posture.rationale: Buyer posture remains ACCUMULATE because average pressure is 39.4 while the only true outlier is energy stress at 67.9; that supports staged buying rather than all-at-once execution.

contractImpactCalculator.title: Contract Impact Calculator
contractImpactCalculator.body: Current posture stays ACCUMULATE. Energy stress at 67.9 and volatility at 44.1 argue for tranche execution because the lead-risk channel is 28.5 points above the average 39.4 pressure baseline.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Quantify staged-buy versus single-window contract execution under current volatility, crush, and macro tail conditions for soybean oil procurement.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: When one dominant risk channel sits materially above the average network score, spreading execution windows lowers timing regret more effectively than seeking a perfect entry.
quantResearchProtocol: Use the average pressure, top-driver score, and top-driver spread as the primary execution-structure inputs.
Classify whether the risk network supports one-shot, staged, or defer-and-watch execution.
Anchor the buyer recommendation in replacement-cost control rather than speculative return seeking.
Require explicit invalidation triggers tied to energy and volatility moderation.
inferenceConstraints: Do not recommend one-shot execution while the lead channel remains materially above the average stack.
Do not describe staging without quantifying why staging is preferred.
Do not present procurement guidance as a trade idea.
outputRequirements: Quote the key score math behind the recommendation.
List the conditions that would justify changing the structure.

sourceRecords.recordHint: average pressure and lead-risk metrics used in execution framing
energy, volatility, and composite synthesis snapshot

notes: Execution guidance is staged because the pressure network is asymmetric, not because the entire system is in panic.
This card is written for a physical buyer managing replacement cost.

factorWaterfall.title: Factor Waterfall
factorWaterfall.body: Current ordering is energy stress 67.9, vix stress 44.1, china tension 37.9, tariff threat 27.4, and crush pressure 19.6. The gap between first and second is 23.8 points, so the stack is not balanced; it is energy-led.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Rank the active drivers by marginal procurement impact and explain causal ordering so purchase cadence can prioritize the right risk channels.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: Ranking matters because a single dominant channel should drive monitoring cadence, while lower-ranked channels determine whether the dominant signal is likely to spread or fade.
quantResearchProtocol: List the full ordered stack and compute the first-to-second and first-to-average score gaps.
Explain whether the regime is concentrated or broad-based.
Tie lower-ranked channels to confirmation risk rather than giving them equal weight.
Convert the ranked stack into practical buyer monitoring priorities.
inferenceConstraints: Do not flatten the stack into a generic mixed-risk statement.
Do not give secondary channels equal urgency when the score gaps are wide.
Do not omit the confirmation role of lower-ranked channels.
outputRequirements: Provide the ordered stack with score math.
Explain what the gaps mean for monitoring priority.
Translate the ranking into buyer action focus.

sourceRecords.recordHint: score inputs for ordered driver stack

notes: Buyer focus should start with the lead channel, then track confirmation risk from the middle of the stack.

riskMetrics.title: Risk Metrics
riskMetrics.body: Average pressure is 39.4, lead pressure is 67.9, and the spread is 28.5. That is a neutral-to-cautious buyer regime: not systemic panic, but too asymmetric to justify passive waiting.

strategicSpecialInstructions.cardTopic: Buyer Risk Math and Tail Exposure
strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Express near-term procurement risk using explicit asymmetry, latency risk, and exposure math so decision timing is grounded in quantified downside.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: Buyer risk is driven less by the mean score than by the asymmetry between the lead threat and the rest of the network, because that gap determines the cost of waiting.
quantResearchProtocol: Compute average pressure, top pressure, and top-minus-average spread every refresh.
Classify the regime as contained, asymmetric, or synchronized stress.
Map asymmetry to decision-latency cost versus over-hedge cost for a physical buyer.
Use posture language that Chris can act on without translation.
inferenceConstraints: Do not call the regime neutral if the asymmetry remains wide.
Do not describe risk without stating what waiting costs.
Do not bury the math in narrative-only wording.
outputRequirements: State the three core metrics and regime label.
Explain why latency risk is or is not dominant.
Provide cadence guidance in plain buyer language.

sourceRecords.recordHint: driver ranking used to interpret spread significance

notes: The card distinguishes asymmetric pressure from full-system crisis.
